coredata.dc:title: Influence of synthesis parameters of N-doped graphene quantum dots and polymer composite layer on the performance of CIGS solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Firoz
Ahmad, Vakeel
Alshahrani, Thamraa
Al-Rasheidi, Masoud
Alanazi, Abdulaziz M.
Irshad, Kashif
Zahir, Md Hasan
Kim, Jae Hyun
coredata.dcterms:subject: CIGS PV cell
N-doped graphene quantum dots
PMMA matrix
Downconverters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance parameters analysis of N-GQD/CIGS solar cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Energy Technology Development Program of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP, Grant No. 20203030010200 ), South Korea. The author T. Alshahrani acknowledges the funding by Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number ( PNURSP2022R1 ), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman and (b) FTIR spectra of N-GQDs.
(a) Absorption and (b) PL emission spectra at λ ex = 450 nm of N-GQDs.
(a) Optical image of the N-GQDs/PMMA coated layer on the quart's substrate under the illumination of UV-radiation (λ ex = 365 nm) and (b) PL emission spectra at λ ex = 405 nm.
Schematic of the downconversion mechanism in the N-GQDs/PMMA layer.
Illuminated J-V characteristics of the CIGS solar cells with N-GQDs/PMMA layer along with bare cell for the comparison.
Dependency of Jsc and Voc values of the CIGS solar cell on the annealing temperature.
Dependency of FF and η values of the CIGS solar cell on the annealing temperature.
Fitting of the illuminated J-V data at (a) short circuit and (b) open circuit conditions for the determination of the values of Rsc and Roc.
Dependency of Jph, Rsh, and Rs values of the CIGS solar cell on the annealing temperature.
Dependency of n and J0 values of the CIGS solar cell on the annealing temperature.
The measured values of the performance parameters of the CIGS solar cells.
Comparison of the graphene quantum dots-based downshifting/converter layer in solar cells.
The predicted values of the PV cell parameters of the CIGS solar cells.

head.ce:title: Influence of synthesis parameters of N-doped graphene quantum dots and polymer composite layer on the performance of CIGS solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Energy Technology, Daegu Gyeongbuk Institute of Science & Technology (DGIST), 333, Techno Jungang-Daero, Hyeonpung-Myeon, Dalseong-Gun, Daegu-42988, South Korea

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to superior absorption and changeable bandgap, copper indium gallium selenide (CIGS) has believed as a prominent absorbing material among the thin film-based photovoltaic (PV) cells [1]. However, parasitic absorption is caused in the short wavelength (λ) region via ZnO and CdS layer. Thus, there is a large gap between the theoretical Shockley–Queisser efficiency (∼33%) and the achieved efficiency [2]. For reducing this gap, several strategies have been used via modification in the electronic properties of the cell or photon downconversion [3–5]. The modification of the electronic properties is a problematic approach. Also, it has detrimental effects on the performance of the cell [4]. However, the photon downconversion approach has advantages because it is easy to implement, and the device structure is disrupted. In this approach, a luminescent down-shifting (LDS) material is implanted on the top of the cell, which converts the low wavelength (λ) photons to the more suitable λ. Though numerous LDS materials, such that organic-inorganic quantum dots (QDs) [6–9], graphene QDs (GQDs) [10–14], organic dyes [15,16], and rare-earth complexes [17–19] are explored. But these LDS are not effective for the CIGS solar cells due to poor photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY), insufficient absorption, or less separation between absorption and emission spectra [20]. Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) are found to be more effective as an LDS layer for the CIGS solar cells, which enhance the PL QY as well as Stokes shift [21,22]. For terrestrial applications, N-GQDs need to be embedded in the transparent polymer matrix [23]. There are several polymers (polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polypyrrole (PPA), polyethylene glycol (PEG), polyaniline (PANI), poly (3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene): poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS), cellulose acetate (CA), poly (vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF)), which are used for embedding the GQDs for various applications [24–29]. Among the above matrixes, PMMA is lightweight and provides good mechanical strength [30]. Moreover, it has less degradation in moisture and offers excellent optical properties [31].

The graphene oxide (GO) powder was used as the starting material for the synthesis of N-GQDs, however, polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW: 25,000) was used N-source. An aqueous solution of GO was prepared via the dispersion of GO power in DI water. For proper dispersion, the GO aqueous solution (2 mg/ml) was sonicated for 5 h dispersed deionized (DI) water followed by sonication for 5 h to get an aqueous solution of GO. Then PEI was also added to the solution and mixed with stirring. This GO/PEI composite was heated at 90 °C for 4 h for the functionalization of the GO. Then the samples were transferred to the horizontal furnace and heat-treated at 300 °C under N2 ambient for various duration (1 h) to synthesize N-GQDs. The NGQD solution was made by sonication of the sample in chlorobenzene followed by filtration using a syringe filter. The PMMA was mixed in the N-GQDs solution to form N-GQDs/PMMA composite. The N-GQDs/PMMA composite solution was spin-coated on the quartz glass. The deposited films were annealed at various temperatures (25, 40, 60, and 80 °C) under N2 ambient. Hereafter, the N-GQDs/PMMA films synthesis at 25, 40, 60, and 80 °C will be referred to as N-GQDs/PMMA-25, N-GQDs/PMMA-40, N-GQDs/PMMA-60, and N-GQDs/PMMA-80, respectively. Moreover, the corresponding CIGS solar cells will be referred to as CIGS-25, CIGS-40, CIGS-60, and CIGS-80, respectively. The CIGS solar cells with uncoated quartz glass are referred to as “Bare” cells.

The Raman spectra were obtained using a confocal Raman spectrometer (Nicolet Almeca XRA, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The value of λ ex was 532 nm for the Raman characterization. A UV–vis–near-infrared spectrophotometer (Carry-5000, Agilent Technologies) was used to obtain the absorbance spectra. The PL spectra were measured using a PL spectrophotometer (Darsa, PSI Trading Co. Ltd.) with a Xe lamp. The excitation wavelength (λ ex) used for PL measurement was 405 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were logged for boding analysis in the N-GQDs using an FTIR spectroscope (Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The solar cell fabrication process was proved elsewhere [21]. The synthesized N-GQDs/polymer composite films were implanted on top of the CIGS PV cells. The J–V characteristics (active area of ∼0.43 cm2) were measured under 1-sun AM1.5G illumination using a source meter (Keithley 2400). The external quantum efficiencies were determined using a PV measuring device in the wavelength range of 300–1100 nm (PV Measurements Incorporated, G1218a). The illumination intensity was calibrated using a Si reference solar cell (PV Measurements Incorporated). All measurements were accomplished at 25 °C.

Raman spectrum of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. The observed peaks cantered at 1257 and 1582 cm−1 corresponding to D and G peaks, respectively [32,33]. The D peak is linked with the deficiencies in the graphene; however, the G peak is associated with dual-degenerated E2g mode the hexagonal lattice of graphene [34]. Moreover, one additional peak is observed at 1414 cm−1, which is related to N-moieties present in N-GQDs. These results confirm the formation of N-GQDs, which are highly functionalized with N-related functional groups. Furthermore, the FTIR spectra of N-GQDs are obtained for bonding analysis as displayed in Fig. 1b. The FTIR absorbance band obtained at 3359.8 and 3248.6 cm−1 are related to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively [35,36]. Two absorbance peaks due to the bond signature of C-H stretching vibration are found at 2935.4 and 2861.8 cm−1 [37]. A strong absorption peak of C=C is obtained at 1635.5 cm−1. There are several absorption peaks related to C=C/C-N in the range of 1460 to 1260 cm−1, which revealed that N atoms effectively doped in the graphitic structure of N-GQDs [35].

The morphology and dimensions of the N-GQDs are determined by the AFM topological characteristics. The AFM topological image of the N-GQDs is displayed in Fig. 2 . The obtained height of the N-GQDs is 3–5 nm [22]. The height of the N-GQDs is slightly higher than the previously reported value; probably it is due to the attachment of a large number of the N-related functional group to the graphitic structure of N-GQDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3 a. A broad absorption is found for the N-NGDs, which included four usual individual absorption bands [38]. The deconvoluted absorption bands are located at 223, 278, 315, and 353 nm. Thus, a broad absorption at λ < 500 nm is obtained. The π→π* transition of C=C (of sp2 C domain in sp3 C matrix) is initiated for the absorption in the high energy UV region (<250 nm). However, the absorption in the higher wavelength region (250–500 nm) is due to the transition of π→π* C=O or C=N, which is caused by absorption in the UV–Vis region. This wide absorption in the wavelength of 300–500 nm is very worthwhile for the efficient LDS material for CIGS solar cells.

The PL spectrum of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3b. The PL emission peak is obtained at 531 nm for the excitation of a wavelength of 450 nm, which provides a Stokes shift of 81 nm. The emission peak position is obtained for the >500 nm. A large Stokes shift is also beneficial for an efficient LDS for the CIGS solar cells.

After the inclusion of the PMMA in the N-GQDs solution, the N-GQDs/PMMA layers are coated onto quartz substrate via spin coating. The N-GQDs/PMMA layers are dried at various temperatures under N2 ambient. The optical images of the N-GQDs/PMMA film annealed at 25 and 60 °C under UV illumination (λ ex = 365 nm) are displayed in Fig. 4 a. The optical image of bare quartz glass is also shown for comparison. The color of the N-GQDs/PMMA films confirms that the wavelength of the emitted light would be ∼490 nm (cyan color). Further, the PL spectra of the N-GQDs/PMMA films were obtained at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 4b that the obtained PL peak position is at λ = ∼500 nm. This result confirms that a slight PL quenching is obtained after making a composite of the N-GQDs with the PMMA. Thus, this film will be very effective as an LDS layer for the CIGS solar cell. The PL intensity of the N-GQDs/PMMA film is initially amplified with the rise in the annealing temperature up to 60 °C. This may be a better link of the N-GQDs with the PMMA layer. However, the PL intensity is shrunken for the annealing temperature of 80 °C. For the potential evaluation of these N-GQDs/PMMA layers, they were applied to the CIGS solar cells. The LDS mechanism of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer is shown in Fig. 5 [39,40]. The photons of wavelengths higher than 500 nm can directly absorb by the CIGS layer to contribute to the current generation. However, the N-GQDs/PMMA down-conversion (DC) layer absorbs the low wavelength photon and re-emits them to the suitable range, which can be easily absorbed by the CIGS. In this way, the illuminated current is increased, which results in the enhancement of the power conversion efficiency of the CIGS solar cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance parameters analysis of N-GQD/CIGS solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: The illuminated current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of the bare and N-GQD/PMMA cells are displayed in Fig. 6 . The performance parameters of the CIGS solar cells were extracted from the illuminated J-V curves. These values are given in Table 1 .

It can be seen that the value of Jsc is increased with the insertion of the DC layer (Fig. 7 a). With the rise in the annealing temperatures, Jsc is found to be enhanced. It attends a maximum value of 36.03 mA/cm2 for the annealing temperature of 60 °C, which is 1.33 mA/cm2 higher than the bare cell (for the bare cell Jsc = 34.05 mA/cm2). Hence, an overall relative enhancement of 3.8% is obtained in the Jsc for the CIGS-60. A similar trend of the variation in the Voc is obtained (Fig. 7b). The highest value of the Voc is obtained for the CIGS-60 (659.2 mV), which is ∼1.6% higher than the Voc value of the bare cell (648.2 mV). The dependencies of the FF and η on the annealing temperature of the N-GQDs/PMMA layer are shown in Fig. 8 a & b, respectively. The FF value is enhanced to a maximal value of 67.91% for CIGS-60, which is 1.30% (absolute) higher than the bare cell (66.61%). Hence, the overall efficiency of the cell is enhanced from 14.70% (bare) to 16.13% for the cell with N-GQDs/PMMA annealed at 60 °C, which is a 1.43% absolute enhancement. This enhancement is due to the photon downshifting as well as reducing the reflection loss from the front surface of the cell [22]. Numerous polymer matrixes are used to embed the GQDs for solar cell application (as illustrated in Table 2 ).

For a better understanding of the losses and improvement in the performance parameters of the cells, the PV cell parameters are predicted using the analytical method [45]. This method is based on a single-diode model, which requires the inverse of the slopes (dJ/dV)−1 at short circuit (Rsc) and open (Roc) circuit conditions [46,47].

where V
m is the voltage at the maximum power point and J
m is the current density at the maximum power point. V
T is thermal voltage (kT/q), k is Boltzmann's constant, T is temperature, and q is the charge of the electron [46,48].

As it can be seen from Fig. 10 a that the trend of variation in the Jph is similar to that of Jsc. The Jph value is increased to a maximal value of 36.68 mA/cm2 for the annealing temperature of 60 °C, which is 7.4% higher than the Jph of the bare cell (34.14 mA/cm2). With the further rise in the annealing temperature, the value of Jph is reduced to 34.69 mA/cm2 at 80 °C. This reduction in the Jph value may be due to PL quenching at 80 °C. The dependency of Rsh on annealing temperature is shown in Fig. 10b. The Rsh value is slightly reduced from 354.61 to 318.47 Ωcm2 via the insertion of N-GQDs/PMMA. However, it is increased with the rise in the annealing temperature and attends a maximal value of 552.49 Ωcm2 for the annealing temperature of 60 °C, which is again reduced to 343.64 Ωcm2 at 80 °C. The improvement in the Rsh with the rise in the annealing temperature is due to the improvement in the Jsc [47]. The value of Rs is found to be increased up to an annealing temperature of 40 °C (Fig. 10c). With the further rise in the annealing temperature, the Rs is slightly reduced. The lowest value of Rs (0.7788 Ωcm2) is obtained at 80 °C, which is slightly lower than the Rs value of the bare cell. The dependencies of the n and J0 are shown in Fig. 11 a & b, respectively. The n is reduced from 2.0094 (bare) to 1.8708 for GIGS-25, which is increased to a maximal value of 2.0101 for the GIGS-60. The value of n is again reduced to 1.9935 for the annealing temperature of 80 °C. The value of J0 is reduced from 1.1413 × 10−7 to 4.0503 × 10−8 A/cm2 for the annealing temperature of 25 °C. It is slightly increased to 5.0953 × 10−8 A/cm2 for the annealing temperature of 40 °C. However, the value of J0 is increased to 9.7431 × 10−8 A/cm2 for the annealing temperature of 60 °C, with further increases in the annealing temperature, no significant change is observed.

The N-GQDs of ultra-high PLQY were synthesized using a thermal process. The PMMA was added to the N-GQDs solution to form N-GQDs/PMMA composite solution. The solution was used to synthesize of N-GQDs/PMMA LDS layer. The best optical and luminescence properties were obtained for the N-GQDs/PMMA layer annealed at 60 °C. The obtained values of Jsc, Voc, FF, and η values of the champion cell were 36.13 mA/cm2, 659.2 mV, 67.91%, and 16.13%, respectively, which is quite higher than the performance parameters of the reference cell (Jsc = 34.04 mA/cm2, Voc = 648.2 mV, FF = 66.61%, and η = 14.70%). These results revealed that 6.13% and 9.73% enhancement was achieved in Jsc and η via insertion of the N-GQDs/PMMA LDS layer. The values of Jph, Rsh, Rs, n, and J0 of the champion cell were 36.06 mA/cm2, 552.49 Ωcm2, 0.7913 Ωcm2, 2.0101, and 9.7231 A/cm2, respectively.

Firoz Khan: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Vakeel Ahmad: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Thamraa Alshahrani: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Masoud Al-Rasheidi: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Abdulaziz M. Alanazi: Investigation, Data curation. Kashif Irshad: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Md Hasan Zahir: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Jae Hyun Kim: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Energy Technology Development Program of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP, Grant No.20203030010200), South Korea. The author T. Alshahrani acknowledges the funding by Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R1), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
